mappings.rationaleAI: Explores prehistoric cultures and their practices including early agriculture and ritual behavior, directly relevant to 821.A on prehistoric religion and 821.A.3 on prehistoric cultural stages.
Examines archaeological methods including interpretation of rock art, Lascaux, and burial sites, directly relevant to 821.A.1 on methods of interpreting prehistoric religion.
Traces the emergence of symbolic behavior in early humans, providing context for the origins of prehistoric religion in 821.A.
Anthropological study of animism, totemism, rites of passage, and shamanism makes this essential reading for 821.B on primitive religion.
Explores traditional religions of indigenous African peoples including cosmology, rites of passage, and sacred practices, directly relevant to 821.B.5.a.
Indigenous peoples of North America maintained rich religious traditions and cultural practices, directly relevant to 821.B.5.c on religions of indigenous peoples of the Americas.
Aztec religion -- with its elaborate sacrificial rites, cosmology, and temple worship -- is central to 821.B.5.c on religions of indigenous peoples of the Americas.
Addresses sacrifice, purification, passage rites, and ritualization, directly relevant to 821.B.4.a on primitive religious practices.
Addresses the nature and function of myth in religion, directly relevant to 821.B.3 on myth and symbol in primitive religion.
Creation myths, flood narratives, trickster figures, and deity archetypes across cultures illuminate 821.B.2.d on primitive cosmogonies and 821.B.3.
Examines magic as a religious and ritual practice, relevant to 821.A.2.f on sorcery and magic and 821.B.4.e on shamanism and divination.
Traces witchcraft beliefs to their roots in primitive and prehistoric religious practice, relevant to 821.A.2.f on sorcery.
Polytheism, nature worship, shamanism, and sacred practices reflecting primitive religious worldviews are central themes, relevant to 821.B.4.b-d.
Druidic religion -- encompassing prophecy, sacrifice, and magic -- preserves elements of prehistoric and primitive religious practice relevant to 821.A.2.c-f.
Examines superstitious beliefs with roots in primitive views of the sacred and profane, relevant to 821.B.2.
Celtic peoples retained religious practices with deep roots in prehistoric religion, illuminating the transition from prehistoric to documented religion.
Ancient Near Eastern cultures with their temples, priests, and festivals bridge prehistoric and early historical religion, relevant to 821.A.3.
The Ice Age environment shaped early prehistoric religious practices including cave art, providing crucial context for 821.A.3.
